clean_story:
Proof’s weight. Who must convince whom.

The term burden of proof comes from law—like a scale where one side must tip first. It’s not about who’s right, but who must show they’re right.

Imagine a game where Player A claims, “There’s a dragon in the garage.” Player B doesn’t believe it. The burden isn’t on B to prove dragons don’t exist—it’s on A to open the garage door. Courts work the same: prosecutors must present evidence; defendants don’t have to prove innocence. In science, a new theory carries the burden until experiments confirm it. Even in debates, the person making the boldest claim holds the weight—like saying “This herb cures all diseases” without trials.

Flip the burden, and truth gets murky. If defenders had to prove they weren’t speeding, every driver would lose. The rule keeps fairness sharp: extraordinary claims demand extraordinary proof.

Three related subjects are legal presumptions, scientific falsifiability, and logical fallacies.
